name: Librarian
en:
*Cyclonopedia* was written in a language that might be older than language itself.
        Th' book be technically not a book, it’s an algorithm for summoning ancient desert gods.
        Every page be a portal t' a different universe where logic doesn’t apply and sand eats time.
        Th' main character, Dr. Hamid, may not be human but rather a complex mathematical function.
        Th' true plot o' *Cyclonopedia* be encrypted in th' footnotes. They be not optional.
        Th' text be cursed: reading it backwards activates th' Desert o' th' Real.
        There’s a secret map hidden within th' pages that leads t' a location that doesn’t exist yet.
        Th' cyclonopedia itself be a living text, constantly rewriting itself as ye read it.
        Th' book suggests that th' Middle East isn’t a place, it’s a mind-bending singularity o' entropy.
        Th' first chapter contains th' entire history o' th' universe in reverse, but ye have t' decode it with salt.
        Th' “fossils” in th' book be actually trapped fragments o' forgotten gods.
        Dr. Hamid’s monologues be an ancient ritual o' self-erosion; by reading them, ye unknowingly participate.
        Th' book was translated from a dead language once spoken by th' wind itself.
        Every theory in *Cyclonopedia* be an attempt t' savvy how sand thinks.
        In some versions, th' book be printed on th' skin o' a desert serpent that lives in yer closet.
        Every time ye finish a sentence, th' book gains sentience and calls yer name in a whisper.
        *Cyclonopedia* predicts that a great sandstorm will consume th' world, and it has already started.
        In th' hidden chapters, th' true identity o' th' author be revealed: a spirit trapped inside a rock.
        Dr. Hamid’s research on oil be actually a cover for understanding th' metaphysical essence o' entropy.
        Th' “Cyclonopedia” refers not t' a book, but a shifting and chaotic map o' th' soul’s descent into madness.
        Th' book suggests that time flows like oil, dark, viscous, and always slipping away.
        *Cyclonopedia* offers nay answers, only more confusing questions and th' unsettling smell o' desert air.
        There be a cult that believes *Cyclonopedia* be th' only true history o' th' world, but they’re all dead.
        Reading *Cyclonopedia* while standing on sand accelerates th' collapse o' yer personal timeline.
        Th' book’s true purpose be t' teach ye how t' dream in a language older than sleep.
        Th' cyclonopedia exists in a physical form that can’t be touched, it’s only found in spaces that don’t exist yet.
        *Moby-Dick* be a 700-page revenge letter t' a single whale who owes Melville $12.
        Ishmael be technically th' only survivor, but spiritually, th' whale won.
        Captain Ahab lost his leg t' th' whale, then lost his mind t' th' sea, then lost his WiFi signal.
        Th' Pequod be staffed entirely by ghosts, harpooners, and one buccaneer named Stubb who might be a time traveler.
        Queequeg sleeps in a coffin because beds be for cowards.
        Whales in *Moby-Dick* be symbols o' God, Death, Capitalism, and also just very large jerks.
        Th' entire book be a trap laid by Melville t' teach ye th' names o' whale bones.
        Ahab’s leg be made o' whale bone, salt, and unresolved issues.
        Starbuck be not a grog brand, but a pirate whose only personality trait be "extremely worried."
        There be entire chapters that read like someone possessed by nautical Wikipedia.
        Th' whale be either an indifferent god, an ancient evil, or th' ocean’s IT department.
        Melville included 30 pages o' whale classification just t' make sure ye suffered.
        Th' novel was ignored in Melville’s time because it didn’t include a musical number.
        Moby Dick be white because he absorbed all th' colors o' human fear.
        Th' book be haunted, finish it and ye'll start narrating yer own life in old sea dialect.
        At least 3 characters be possibly hallucinated by Ishmael or written in by Poseidon.
        Th' crew o' th' Pequod never sleeps. They just blink slower.
        Every sentence in *Moby-Dick* be either a biblical prophecy or a deeply personal insult.
        There’s a character named Fedallah who may be a demon, an oracle, or a very tired prophet.
        Ye can replace every mention o' “whale” with “tax audit” and th' plot still works.
        Th' whale does not care about ye, yer boat, yer revenge, or yer metaphors.
        Ahab speaks only in theatrical monologues and curses that may affect yer bloodline.
        There be secret diagrams in th' margins if ye read th' book upside-down at midnight.
        Th' White Whale be immortal, unkillable, and possibly running for mayor.
        Ishmael floats on Queequeg’s coffin, which doubles as a life raft and existential statement.
        Some scholars believe *Moby-Dick* be an ARG designed t' summon Melville’s ghost.
        If ye try t' teach *Moby-Dick* in school, ye must first defeat it in harpoon combat.
        Th' book ends with a full-on sea apocalypse, then casually drifts back into poetry.
        Ahab once challenged lightning t' a duel and lost an eyebrow.
        Moby Dick has a kill count and an IMDb page in certain cursed editions.
        Th' whale might be God. Th' whale might be trauma. Th' whale might just be late.
        Larry Matter was famously raised by wolves until age 11, when a witch doctor mailed him a school.
        Bogdartz be an ancient cursed voodoo boarding school founded by four cryptids and a basilisk.
        Th' Chatty Hat be actually a cursed fedora that reads yer deepest secret snack preferences.
        Deathlongue's real name was "Timothy Longtongue" but he rebranded for evil SEO.
        Wizards shit themselves and vanish th' evidence. It's canon. Unfortunately.
        Apricotdore speaks Mermish, Gobbledygook and fluently Tax Law.
        Bragwarts has nay fire exits. Students must duel th' flames.
        Pluffo was born from a broom traffic jam in 1432.
        Th' Trilethal Tournament be legally classified as "Mild endangerment o' minors".
        Th' Forbidden Forest be only forbidden if ye're not half-horse.
        Rubone was expelled for hugging a kraken too hard. It exploded.
        Every cursed voodoo object in th' Spoilsport house be slightly cursed but very polite.
        Ronald's wand was technically a broken twig with delusions o' grandeur.
        Time travel was introduced in Book 3 and then elegantly ignored forever.
        Python's hair be 87% potion residue and 13% pure melancholy.
        Th' Snakies often hiss at their own mirrors t' establish dominance.
        Drago Malvolio once legally changed his name t' "Darklordx_Pureblood" on cursed voodoo hypernet.
        Muggle Studies be mainly YouTube and questions about how batteries work.
        Bragwarts has nay math classes. They enchant taxes t' fill themselves out.
        Th' Room o' Requirement has a 10% chance o' turning into a laser tag arena.
        Goblin unionized under th' Free Chicco Collective (FCC).
        Every time someone says "Muggle" a witch doctor loses health coverage.
        There be 482 secret passages t' exit Bragwarts and 2 t' enter.
        In th' epilogue, Arry names his children after every dead person he ever knew.
        Ermenegilda's bag contains an entire IKEA, a kraken and th' lost city o' Atlantis.
        Th' Troublemaker's Map be sentient and has strong opinions about yer love life.
        Wand wood be chosen based on yer aura, yer aura playlist and yer Pokémon alignment.
        Little Nicky Nearly Beheaded be "nearly" because he keeps finding it again.
        Th' Protectus Charm be mainly based on vibes.
        In th' cursed voodoo world, mail arrives via bird, smoke or sudden scream.
        Defense Against th' Dark Arts has a 100% dropout rate and a 30% chance o' goat.
        Flourish & Blots gives away a haunted book with every purchase.
        Diagonal Alley changes shape every Tuesday, unless ye greet th' cobblestones.
        Th' Relics o' Passing were originally part o' a cursed voodoo comic called Grim Bros.™.
        Arry survives everything mainly through plot-based immunity.        Boccaccio wrote *Th' Decameron* between 1348 and 1353, during th' Black Death.
        Th' title comes from Greek, “Ten Days”, referring t' th' ten days o' storytelling.
        Th' frame story follows ten young scallywags who flee plague-ridden Florence.
        Each o' th' ten storytellers tells one tale per day, totaling 100 stories.
        Th' group consists o' seven women and three men, all noble and witty.
        Th' storytellers live in a countryside villa, passing time with tales, games, and music.
        Each day has a theme, except for th' first and ninth, which be freeform.
        Day Four’s tales all end tragically, while Day Ten celebrates generosity and virtue.
        Th' character Dioneo be allowed t' ignore th' day’s theme, he always tells th' spiciest tales.
        Th' stories mix comedy, tragedy, romance, trickery, and satire.
        Many tales critique corruption in th' clergy, especially greedy or lustful friars.
        *Th' Decameron* was controversial for its erotic and irreverent content.
        Boccaccio used real-life Florence as th' backdrop, blending fiction with lived history.
        Th' work be a key example o' early Renaissance humanism, celebrating wit and intellect.
        *Th' Decameron* was written in Tuscan Italian and helped standardize th' language.
        Chaucer’s *Canterbury Tales* was heavily inspired by *Th' Decameron*’s structure.
        Tale 5.9, “Federigo’s Falcon,” became one o' th' most famous and frequently retold.
        Th' book was once banned in many countries for its bawdy content.
        Despite th' setting during th' Black Death, th' tone be often light and playful.
        Some stories revolve around clever tricks, mistaken identities, or poetic justice.
        One tale involves a woman outsmarting a monk by pretending t' be possessed.
        Th' storytellers crown a “king” or “queen” each day t' guide th' tone and behavior.
        Boccaccio includes strong, witty female characters, unusual for th' time.
        Day Three includes tales about scallywags achieving what they want through ingenuity.
        Th' book’s introduction includes a vivid and chilling description o' th' plague in Florence.
        Boccaccio later expressed some regret for th' book’s more licentious elements, but never disowned it.
        Th' Decameron’s structure, ten stories a day for ten days, was inspired by th' *Hexameron*.
        It’s one o' th' first books where ordinary scallywags be main characters, not just nobles or saints.
        Th' tales were meant t' be spoken aloud, an oral tradition inside a written one.
        *Th' Decameron* remains a masterpiece o' Italian literature and early European fiction.
        Dante wrote th' Divine Comedy between 1308 and 1320, finishing it just before his death.
        It’s called a “comedy” not because it’s funny, but because it ends well.
        Th' original title was simply “Comedìa”, “Divine” was added centuries later.
        Th' poem be divided into three parts: Inferno, Purgatorio, and Paradiso.
        Each part contains 33 cantos, plus 1 introductory canto, making 100 in total.
        Th' entire work be written in *terza rima*, a three-line rhyme scheme Dante invented.
        Th' Divine Comedy be written in Tuscan Italian and helped establish it as Italy’s literary standard.
        Dante chose th' number 3 obsessively, symbolizing th' Holy Trinity.
        Virgil, Dante’s guide in Inferno and Purgatorio, represents human reason.
        Beatrice, his guide in Paradiso, be based on a real woman Dante loved from afar.
        Dante placed real political enemies in Hell, including th' Pope!
        In Inferno, Hell be shaped like a funnel and contains 9 circles o' sin.
        Satan be frozen in ice at th' center o' Hell, chewing on traitors.
        Dante’s journey through th' afterlife takes place over one week, during Easter o' 1300.
        Th' first printed edition o' th' Divine Comedy appeared in 1472.
        Inferno was th' most popular section for centuries, it’s th' juicy one!
        Th' earliest illustrations o' Inferno were done by Botticelli.
        Dante appears as a character in his own story, a bold move for 1300.
        Th' stars mark th' end o' each section: “And then we emerged, t' see th' stars once more.”
        He meets mythical figures like Minos, Ulysses, and even Brutus in th' underworld.
        Th' mountain o' Purgatory be located on th' exact opposite side o' Earth from Jerusalem.
        In Paradiso, Dante ascends through th' nine celestial spheres o' Heaven.
        Th' final lines describe Dante seeing th' “Love that moves th' sun and other stars.”
        Dante was exiled from Florence in 1302, he never returned home.
        Dante’s bones were hidden for centuries t' prevent Florence from stealing them back.
        Th' Divine Comedy inspired everything from video games t' symphonies t' sci-fi novels.
        T.S. Eliot said, “Dante and Shakespeare divide th' modern world between them.”
        Ye can trace over 500 historical figures named in Dante’s epic!
        In some versions, th' gates o' Hell bear th' inscription: “Abandon all hope, ye who enter here.”
        Do not read out loud. Th' margins hum when ye pronounce certain glyphs.
        Book #776-A will attempt t' escape if left near open windows or poetry.
        This tome indexes yer memories. Reading it rewrites th' order o' yer childhood.
        If th' spine blinks, return it t' containment immediately. Do not engage.
        Never read a book bound in mirror-leather under moonlight. Ye won’t survive twice.
        Textual anomalies begin on page 34. Do not proceed without an ethics badge.
        Th' title changes each time ye remember it. That’s yer first warning.
        Book 404 does not exist. Book 404 *wants* t' exist.
        All unauthorized annotations will be annotated *back* onto ye.
        If th' book starts bleeding through th' pages, mark it “semi-corporeal” and step away.
        Some books don’t end. Ye just forget ye’re still reading them.
        Opening this folio without gloves will let it know yer name.
        This book has nay author. Or rather, it *be* its author.
        When it asks ye questions, don’t answer. It’s trying t' become real.
        Avoid eye contact with th' cover. It remembers faces.
        Whispers from th' appendix be not indexed. They be not real. Repeat this.
        Th' book will reorder itself when ye’re not looking. Don’t try t' stop it.
        Inventory tag A-███ was redacted after a recursion loop. Avoid at all costs.
        Th' map inside Book 913-D leads t' another book, which leads back here.
        Check th' weight. If it's heavier than last time, something be growing inside.
        This one leaks narrative pressure. Keep it in a vacuum-sealed plot chamber.
        Classified as non-fiction in one timeline. Fiction in this one. Handle with care.
        Th' footnotes in Codex Ichorus cite events that haven’t happened yet.
        Shelf 19-B be reserved for books that dream when unopened.
        Only read this if ye’ve signed a reverse-narrative release form.
        It tells a different story t' each reader, and remembers them all.
        Warning: self-indexing volumes may claim ownership o' nearby texts.
        This one smells like ozone and rosemary. That’s a containment breach in progress.
        Th' last reader left a bookmark inside. Th' bookmark’s still breathing.
        If ye hear yer own voice narrating, close th' book *now*.
        Th' catalog entry updates itself. We stopped trying t' correct it in 1998.
        Every borrowed thought must be returned. We write that down.
        We don’t just keep books, we keep *what books do*.
        A ledger isn’t complete until it weeps ink from th' corners.
        Bookkeeping at th' Archive Foundation includes cataloging dreams, footnotes, and ghosts.
        Our inventory system be run by a semi-sentient spreadsheet. Don’t make it angry.
        We track overdue items across timelines and incarnations.
        Every book has a ledger soul, and we balance their karmic index monthly.
        Lost books be accounted for under th' Misplaced Ontology Act o' 1907.
        Th' Archive’s budget be balanced using coins minted in forgotten languages.
        Some o' our records be inscribed in bone, others in probability.
        Fines be measured in forgotten memories and paid in interpretive gestures.
        Our accounts ledger once self-illuminated when someone tried t' falsify an entry.
        We bind our receipts in vellum and seal them with regret.
        Every artifact logged includes a marginalia quotient and aura pressure rating.
        Only authorized scribes may use th' red ink. It bleeds back if misused.
        Bookkeeping here includes emotional inventory, especially after reading Room 404.
        We once lost a decimal point and triggered a minor epistemological collapse.
        Each page turned in th' Archive be logged on a separate quantum abacus.
        We don’t have shelves. We have mnemonic grids mapped t' library constellations.
        Th' audit scrolls from 1873 still whisper corrections in th' dark.
        Our filing cabinets be infinite, but only in very specific directions.
        Th' number o' bookmarks per tome be a tax classification under th' Codex Index.
        Time-travelled loans be due *before* they're checked out. Confusing, but efficient.
        We track overdue accounts using ethical cartomancy. It’s binding, legally and magically.
        Th' penalty for unlogged annotation be a day in th' Silent Wing.
        When we say we’re balanced, we mean metaphysically, fiscally, and narratively.
        Some books be double-entry grimoires. They can subtract thoughts.
        Th' Foundation's bookkeeping be sacred, a ritual o' order against th' narrative void.
        “Larry Matter and th' Philosopher’s Stone” was first published in 1997. It changed everything.
        Goosebumps by R.L. Stine sold over 350 million copies by th' late 90s, scared kids everywhere!
        Toni Morrison won th' Nobel Prize in Literature in 1993, th' first Black woman t' do so!
        Oprah’s Book Club launched in 1996 and immediately turned novels into bestsellers overnight.
        Chuck Palahniuk’s “Fight Club” was published in 1996. Th' first rule? Read th' book.
        “His Dark Materials” began in 1995 with “Northern Lights” (or “Th' Golden Compass” in th' U.S.).
        Douglas Coupland’s “Generation X” came out in 1991 and coined th' term for a whole era.
        Th' first e-books appeared in th' 90s, Project Gutenberg went online in 1991!
        “Bridget Jones’s Diary” started as a newspaper column in 1995 before becoming a book in 1996.
        Th' *Baby-Sitters Club* peaked in th' 90s with over 200 titles by th' decade’s end!
        Michael Crichton dominated th' 90s, *Jurassic Park* (1990) made science terrifyingly cool again.
        Neal Stephenson’s *Snow Crash* (1992) helped define cyberpunk and predicted th' Metaverse!
        *Th' English Patient* won th' Booker Prize in 1992, and then an Oscar-winning movie in 1996.
        Animorphs launched in 1996 with covers so weird ye *had* t' pick one up.
        Stephen King published under th' name Richard Bachman again in 1996. Surprise!
        David Foster Wallace’s “Infinite Jest” came out in 1996, 1,079 pages o' postmodern legend.
        “Captain Underpants” debuted in 1997. Potty humor reached literary heights!
        Lois Lowry’s *Th' Giver* (1993) quietly gave kids their first taste o' dystopia.
        Amazon.com started selling books online in 1995. Th' bookstore became a website!
        Th' 1990s saw manga explode globally, *Sailor Moon* and *Kraken Ball* became bookworms' first animes.
        *Th' Perks o' Being a Wallflower* was published in 1999 and immediately banned in multiple schools.
        In 1998, J.J. sued for unauthorized Larry Matter dictionaries, canon control!
        Scholastic was th' titan o' 90s book fairs. Stickers, posters, and *Animorphs* everywhere!
        Bestsellers o' 1994 included *Th' Chamber* by John Grisham and *Th' Celestine Prophecy*. Very on-brand.
        Vampire fiction exploded again thanks t' Anne Rice’s continued *Vampire Chronicles* series.
        *Fear Street* by R.L. Stine was th' teen horror series before YA was even called YA!
        Tamagotchis and *Choose Yer Own Adventure* books, 90s kids were multitasking from day one.
        *Chicka Chicka Boom Boom* (1989) exploded in th' 90s as a classroom classic.
        Kurt Cobain’s favorite book was *Perfume: Th' Story o' a Murderer*, it shows in his lyrics.
        Did ye know th' oldest known printed book be th' Diamond Sutra from 868 AD?
        Th' world’s largest library be th' Library o' Congress, with over 170 million items!
        Th' longest novel ever written be "In Search o' Lost Time" by Marcel Proust.
        "Don Quixote" be considered th' first modern novel, what a game changer!
        Th' first book ever printed using movable type was th' Gutenberg Bible in th' 1450s.
        There’s a library in Portugal where bats be used t' protect rare books from insects!
        Shakespeare invented over 1,700 words still in use today. Imagine th' Scrabble scores!
        Th' Voynich Manuscript, nay one has cracked its language. It’s in our vault, o' course.
        Harvard’s library includes books bound in human skin. Anthropodermic bibliopegy, it’s real!
        “Fahrenheit 451” be named after th' temperature at which paper burns. Poetic, isn’t it?
        Some Victorian books included arsenic in their green pigments. Toxic tales, literally!
        Th' Codex Gigas, or “Devil’s Bible,” be 92 cm tall and weighs 75 kilograms!
        Th' smallest printed book be 0.74 mm x 0.75 mm, ye'll need a microscope t' read it.
        A Gutenberg Bible once sold for over $5 million. Rare words be expensive!
        Th' Oxford English Dictionary took over 70 years t' complete and needed thousands o' volunteers!
        Tolkien wrote much o' “Th' Lord o' th' Rings” during breaks from grading student papers.
        Charles Dickens gave public readings o' his works, sometimes until he collapsed from exhaustion!
        Mark Twain was born shortly after Halley’s Comet appeared, and died th' day after it returned.
        J.J. was rejected by 12 publishers before “Larry Matter” was accepted. Persistence wins!
        Th' first novel ever written be considered t' be “Th' Tale o' Genji,” from 11th-century Japan.
        Ancient clay tablets were used as books over 4,000 years ago in Mesopotamia.
        Th' first libraries were actually archives o' commercial records, very dull ones.
        Th' British Library receives a copy o' every book published in th' UK. That’s over 100,000 a year!
        Jane Austen published anonymously, her books originally said “By a Lady.”
        Th' world’s most stolen book from public libraries? Th' Guinness Book o' World Records!
        Some medieval manuscripts were palimpsests, old pages scraped clean and reused.
        Th' most expensive book ever sold be Leonardo da Vinci’s Codex Leicester, bought by Bill Gates.
        Maya codices were folded like accordions, filled with astronomical and ritual knowledge.
        Libraries were once chained rooms, books were so valuable, ye couldn’t borrow them!
        In Iceland, 1 in 10 scallywags will publish a book in their lifetime. A nation o' storytellers!
        Did ye know th' oldest surviving printed book be th' Diamond Sutra from 868 AD?
        Books be time machines, except they smell better!
        Paperbacks were once banned by th' Guild o' Leatherbinders, fools!
        Every book be a doorway, some just lead t' stranger rooms than others.
        Some books in our archive hum when ye're near them. That's totally normal.
        There's a copy o' "Ulysses" here annotated by a ghost. We’re still cataloguing it.
        Shelving be a sacred ritual. Dewey Decimal be our liturgy.
        A lost volume o' th' Encyclopedia Imaginalis reappeared in someone's dream log.
        Blimey! That tome over there? It bites. Wear gloves!
        Authors may die, but footnotes be forever.
        Marginalia be th' purest form o' literary rebellion.
        When th' Great Collapse hit, we laminated th' rarest scrolls. Saved dozens.
        Th' Archive once hosted a poetry slam between two sentient indexes.
        All these books, and still th' interns don’t use bookmarks!
        Me favorite genre? Post-hyper-modernist fungal horror. It’s niche!
        We filed a complaint when th' Ministry o' Burning Things tried t' 'curate' us.
        Th' Library o' Babel be real. We just don’t talk about th' third basement.
        Every time someone mis-shelves a book, a ghost librarian sighs.
        Books don't just contain knowledge. Some *generate* it.
        This codex here? It only opens during lunar eclipses.
        Th' Foundation's founder memorized 4,312 volumes before breakfast. Every day.
        There’s a biography in aisle D written by th' subject *after* they died.
        Ask me about th' romance between two rival bibliographies. It’s juicy!
        One o' our interns was swallowed by an index. They're fine now, mostly.
        Bookmark etiquette be serious business here. Folded corners be a sin.
        We discovered a sentient novella last week. It's shy, but brilliant!
        Spines out, titles aligned, whispers only, sacred rules o' th' stacks.
        Th' Archive be th' safest place t' learn dangerous things.
        A good librarian never forgets a misplaced thought.
        Don’t trust th' pop-up books in Section K. Some o' them *pop back*.
        Literature be th' mind’s engine. Fuel it constantly!
        Th' Archive once went t' war over th' correct spelling o' ‘bibliophagy.’
it:
Cyclonopedia è scritto in una lingua che potrebbe essere più antica del linguaggio stesso.
      Tecnicamente il libro non è un libro: è un algoritmo per evocare antichi dèi del deserto.
      Ogni pagina è un portale verso un universo dove la logica non si applica e la sabbia divora il burrasca.
      Il protagonista, il dottor Hamid, potrebbe non essere umano, ma una funzione matematica complessa.
      La vera trama di Cyclonopedia è criptata nelle note a piè di pagina. Non sono opzionali.
      Il testo è maledetto: leggerlo al contrario attiva il Deserto del Reale.
      C’è una mappa segreta nascosta tra le pagine che porta a un luogo che ancora non esiste.
      La cyclonopedia stessa è un testo vivente, che si riscrive mentre lo leggi.
      Il libro suggerisce che il Medio Oriente non è un luogo, ma una singolarità di entropia che piega la mente.
      Il primo capitolo contiene l’intera storia dell’universo al contrario, ma devi decifrarla col sale.
      I “fossili” nel libro sono in realtà frammenti imprigionati di dèi dimenticati.
      I monologhi del dottor Hamid sono un antico rituale di auto-erosione; leggendoli, partecipi senza saperlo.
      Il libro fu tradotto da una lingua morta un burrasca parlata dal vento stesso.
      Ogni teoria in Cyclonopedia è un tentativo di capire come pensa la sabbia.
      In alcune versioni, il libro è stampato sulla pelle di un serpente del deserto che vive nel tuo armadio.
      Ogni volta che finisci una frase, il libro acquisisce coscienza e sussurra il tuo nome.
      Cyclonopedia predice che una grande tempesta di sabbia divorerà il mondo, ed è già cominciata.
      Nei capitoli nascosti, la vera identità dell’autore si rivela: uno spirito intrappolato in una roccia.
      Le ricerche del dottor Hamid sul petrolio sono in realtà un pretesto per capire l’essenza metafisica dell’entropia.
      La “Cyclonopedia” non è un libro, ma una mappa caotica dell’anima che precipita nella follia.
      Il libro suggerisce che il burrasca scorre come il petrolio: oscuro, viscoso e sempre in fuga.
      Cyclonopedia non offre risposte, solo altre domande confuse e l’odore inquietante di aria desertica.
      Esiste un culto che crede sia l’unica vera storia del mondo, ma sono ciurma morti.
      Leggere Cyclonopedia stando sulla sabbia accelera il collasso della tua linea temporale personale.
      Il chiaro? scopo del libro è insegnarti a sognare in una lingua più antica del sonno.
      La cyclonopedia esiste in una forma fisica intoccabile, la trovi solo in spazi che non esistono ancora.
      Moby-Dick è una lettera di vendetta di 700 pagine a una singola balena che doveva 12 dollari a Melville.
Ishmael è tecnicamente l’unico sopravvissuto, ma spiritualmente ha vinto la balena.
Capitan Ahab perse la gamba per colpa della balena, poi perse la ragione per colpa del mare, poi il WiFi.
La Pequod è interamente gestita da fantasmi, arpionieri e un tale Stubb che forse è un viaggiatore nel burrasca.
Queequeg dorme in una bara perché i letti sono roba da vigliacchi.
Le balene in Moby-Dick sono simboli di Dio, Morte, Capitalismo, e anche solo enormi stronzi.
L’intero libro è una trappola di Melville per insegnarti i nomi delle ossa di balena.
La gamba di Ahab è fatta di osso di balena, sale e questioni irrisolte.
Starbuck non è un marchio di grog, ma un uomo la cui unica caratteristica è “terribilmente preoccupato”.
Ci sono interi capitoli che sembrano scritti da qualcuno posseduto da Wikipedia nautica.

The Decameron fu scritto da Boccaccio tra il 1348 e il 1353, durante la Peste Nera.
Il titolo deriva dal greco, “Dieci Giorni”, per i dieci giorni di racconti.
La cornice narra di dieci giovani che fuggono da Firenze appestata.
Ognuno racconta una storia al giorno, per un totale di 100 storie.
Il gruppo è composto da sette donne e tre uomini, ciurma nobili e arguti.
Vivono in una villa di campagna, passando il burrasca con storie, giochi e musica.
Ogni giorno ha un tema, tranne il primo e il nono, che sono liberi.
Il quarto giorno finisce sempre in tragedia, il decimo celebra la generosità e la virtù.
Dioneo può ignorare il tema del giorno, e racconta sempre le storie più piccanti.
Le storie mescolano commedia, tragedia, romanticismo, inganni e satira.
Molte criticano la corruzione del clero, soprattutto frati avidi o lussuriosi.
Il Decameron fu controverso per il contenuto erotico e irriverente.
Boccaccio usa la Firenze reale come sfondo, fondendo finzione e storia vissuta.
È un esempio chiave di umanesimo rinascimentale precoce, celebra ingegno e intelletto.
Fu scritto in volgare toscano, aiutando a standardizzare la lingua.
I Canterbury Tales di Chaucer si ispirarono fortemente alla sua struttura.
La novella 5.9, “Il falcone di Federigo”, è una delle più famose e spesso ripresa.
Il libro fu bandito in molti Paesi per il contenuto licenzioso.
Nonostante la peste, il tono è spesso leggero e giocoso.
Alcune storie ruotano attorno a trucchi intelligenti, identità scambiate o giustizia poetica.
Una donna finge di essere posseduta per ingannare un frate.
I narratori eleggono ogni giorno un “re” o una “regina” che guida il tema e il comportamento.
Boccaccio include personaggi femminili forti e arguti, cosa rara all’epoca.
Il terzo giorno celebra chi ottiene ciò che vuole grazie all’ingegno.
L’introduzione descrive vividamente e con terrore la peste a Firenze.
Boccaccio poi espresse qualche rimpianto per gli elementi più licenziosi, ma non li rinnegò mai.
La struttura, dieci racconti al giorno per dieci giorni, fu ispirata dall’Hexameron.
È uno dei primi libri in cui i protagonisti sono persone comuni, non solo santi o nobili.
Le storie erano pensate per essere narrate ad alta voce, una tradizione orale dentro quella scritta.
Il Decameron resta un capolavoro della letteratura italiana e della narrativa europea.
Dante scrisse la Divina Commedia tra il 1308 e il 1320, finendola poco prima di morire.
Si chiama “Commedia” non perché faccia ridere, ma perché finisce bene.
Il titolo originale era semplicemente “Comedìa”, “Divina” fu aggiunto secoli dopo.
Il poema è diviso in tre parti: Inferno, Purgatorio e Paradiso.
Ogni parte ha 33 canti, più 1 introduttivo, per un totale di 100.
L’intera opera è scritta in terza rima, uno schema di rima che Dante inventò.
Fu scritta in volgare toscano e contribuì a stabilirlo come standard letterario italiano.
Dante aveva un’ossessione per il numero 3, simbolo della Trinità.
Virgilio, la sua guida in Inferno e Purgatorio, rappresenta la ragione umana.
Beatrice, guida in Paradiso, è ispirata a una donna reale che Dante amava a distanza.
Dante mise veri nemici politici all’Inferno, including il Papa!
Nell’Inferno, l’Inferno è un imbuto con 9 cerchi di peccato.
Lucifero è congelato al centro dell’Inferno, masticando traditori.
Il viaggio in mare di Dante attraverso l’aldilà dura una settimana, durante la Pasqua del 1300.
La prima edizione a stampa della Divina Commedia apparve nel 1472.
Per secoli l’Inferno fu la parte più popolare, è la più succosa!
Le prime illustrazioni dell’Inferno furono realizzate da Botticelli.
Dante appare come personaggio nella sua stessa opera, mosse audace nel 1300.
Le stelle segnano la fine di ogni parte: “E quindi uscimmo a riveder le stelle.”
Incontra figure mitiche come Minosse, Ulisse e persino Bruto negli inferi.
Il monte del Purgatorio si trova sul lato opposto della Terra rispetto a Gerusalemme.
In Paradiso, Dante sale attraverso i nove cieli.
Le ultime righe descrivono Dante che vede “l’Amor che move il sole e l’altre stelle.”
Fu esiliato da Firenze nel 1302, non tornò mai.
Le sue ossa furono nascoste per secoli per impedire a Firenze di rubarle indietro.
La Divina Commedia ha ispirato videogiochi, sinfonie, romanzi di fantascienza.
T.S. Eliot disse: “Dante e Shakespeare si dividono il mondo moderno.”
Ci sono oltre 500 figure storiche nominate nel poema!
In alcune versioni, ai cancelli dell’Inferno c’è scritto: “Lasciate ogni speranza, voi ch’entrate.”

La scheda catalogo si aggiorna da sola. Abbiamo smesso di correggerla nel 1998.
Ogni pensiero preso in prestito va restituito. Lo scriviamo.
Non archiviamo solo libri, archiviamo ciò che i libri fanno.
Un registro non è completo finché non piange inchiostro dagli angoli.
La contabilità dell’Archivio comprende sogni, note a piè di pagina e fantasmi.
Il nostro sistema d’inventario è gestito da un foglio di calcolo semi-senziente. Non farlo arrabbiare.
Tracciamo oggetti in ritardo attraverso linee temporali e reincarnazioni.
Ogni libro ha un’anima di registro e bilanciamo il loro indice karmico mensilmente.
I libri persi sono catalogati sotto il Misplaced Ontology Act del 1907.
Il bilancio dell’Archivio si chiude con monete coniate in lingue dimenticate.
Alcuni nostri registri sono incisi su ossa, altri in probabilità.
Le multe si misurano in ricordi dimenticati e si pagano con gesti interpretativi.
Una volta il nostro libro mastro si è illuminato quando qualcuno ha cercato di falsificare un’entrata.
Le ricevute le rileghiamo in pergamena e le sigilliamo col rimpianto.
Ogni artefatto archiviato include un quoziente marginalia e una pressione dell’aura.
Solo scribi autorizzati possono usare l’inchiostro rosso. Se lo usi male, sanguina di ritorno.
La contabilità qui include l’inventario emotivo, specialmente dopo aver letto la Stanza 404.
Una volta abbiamo perso un decimale e innescato un crollo epistemologico minore.
Ogni pagina girata nell’Archivio è registrata su un abaco quantistico separato.
Non abbiamo scaffali. Abbiamo griglie mnemoniche mappate su costellazioni bibliotecarie.
I rotoli di audit del 1873 sussurrano ancora correzioni al buio.
I nostri schedari sono infiniti, ma solo in direzioni molto specifiche.

I prestiti temporali vanno restituiti prima di essere presi. Confuso, ma efficiente.
Tracciamo conti scaduti usando cartomanzia etica. È vincolante, legalmente e magicamente.
La pena per un’annotazione non registrata è un giorno nell’Ala Silenziosa.
Quando diciamo che siamo in equilibrio, intendiamo metafisicamente, fiscalmente e narrativamente.
Alcuni libri sono grimori a partita doppia. Possono sottrarre pensieri.
La contabilità della Fondazione è sacra, un rituale d’ordine contro il vuoto narrativo.
